content.text: Installing & Configuring the Anthropic SDK in Expo
Adding the Anthropic SDK to an Expo project requires more care than a Node.js backend because React Native runs in a JavaScript engine (Hermes) that lacks Node.js built-ins like

, and native crypto. The Metro bundler also resolves packages differently from webpack or esbuild. This guide walks through a production-ready setup: installing the SDK, configuring Metro, handling API keys safely, and making your first call.

attrs.alt: Expo SDK architecture: Expo app calls a backend proxy which holds the API key and forwards requests to Anthropic API
attrs.title: Anthropic SDK setup in Expo — recommended architecture

content.text: Start with a fresh Expo project using the TypeScript template. The
template includes Expo Router which handles navigation — suitable for most Claude-powered apps.

npx create-expo-app@latest my-claude-app --template blank-typescript
cd my-claude-app

# Verify the project boots on simulator / device
npx expo start

npm install @anthropic-ai/sdk

# Also needed for streaming responses in React Native
npm install react-native-fetch-api
npm install react-native-polyfill-globals
package targets both Node.js and the browser. Expo uses the browser entry point (via the
), which relies on the Fetch API. Expo's built-in Fetch polyfill covers this for most use cases, but streaming requires
Metro is Expo's JavaScript bundler. By default it does not resolve the
, which the Anthropic SDK uses to select its browser build. Enable it in
became stable in Metro 0.80 (bundled with Expo SDK 51+). For older Expo SDKs, use the older flag name
Unlike a backend service, your Expo bundle is distributed to every user's device. Any value embedded in JavaScript source — including
values baked in at build time — can be extracted with basic tooling. Never put your
Route all Anthropic calls through your own server. The server holds the API key; the app only knows your server's URL. This pattern also lets you add authentication, rate limiting, and logging.
prefix is safe for non-secret values (server URL) — it tells Expo to inline the value at build time. Never use this prefix for the actual API key.
Option B: Direct SDK Call for Development / Prototyping
For a development-only prototype where you control the device, you can call the SDK directly — but only if the key stays out of source control and never ships in a production build.
Switch to the proxy pattern before shipping to the App Store or Play Store.
variables are bundled into the release binary and are trivially extractable.
to get incremental tokens, Hermes' built-in

Streaming lets Claude's response appear token by token — essential for perceived performance in chat UIs. After applying the polyfill above:
Expo's environment variable system differs from Node.js:
Non-secret config: proxy URL, feature flags
API keys used at build time (not bundled)
For EAS Builds, set secrets with:

# Set an EAS secret (stored server-side, not in the bundle)
eas secret:create --scope project --name ANTHROPIC_API_KEY --value sk-ant-...

# List secrets
eas secret:list
or the Node.js entry point is loaded instead of the browser one. Fix: add
TypeError: stream[Symbol.asyncIterator] is not a function
in the entry point before any SDK imports.
Symptom: calls work but the key can be extracted from the APK/IPA with
. Fix: use a backend proxy for all production builds.
Android 9+ blocks cleartext (HTTP) traffic. All API calls must use HTTPS. Both the Anthropic API and your proxy must serve over TLS — this is normally the case but worth verifying in development.
Hermes does not support all ES2023+ syntax.
The SDK is transpiled for broad compatibility. If you see syntax errors, ensure you are on Expo SDK 50+ which ships Hermes 0.13 with strong ES2022 support.

on the unmounted component logs a warning. Use an AbortController and cancel in the
After following the steps above, run this checklist before moving on to feature development:
— Metro should start without module resolution errors.
Navigate to the screen with your test call. The response should appear (or stream in) without a crash.
Open the Expo developer menu → Network inspector and confirm the request goes to your proxy (not directly to
from the device) in production builds.
and verify the API key is not present in the APK:
so Metro resolves the SDK's browser entry point.
value is baked into the release binary and is extractable.
Use a backend proxy for all production traffic. The proxy holds the key, adds auth, and can enforce rate limits.
at the app entry point before any SDK import.
to cancel in-flight requests when a component unmounts.
